elements.sent-2411-001: It is known that, given a left continuous t-norm $T$ on $[0,1]$ and a right continuous $\CL$-operation $L$ on $[0,\infty]$, then their tensor product $L\otimes T$ is a triangle function on $\Delp$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Hence, $(\Delp,L\otimes T)$ becomes a partially ordered monoid.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, it is shown that: (1) $L\otimes T$ coincides with the triangle function $\tau_{T,L}$ if and only if $L$ satisfies the condition $(LCS)$; (2) let $\CDp$ be the set of all non-defective distance distribution functions, then $(\CDp,L\otimes T)$ is a submonoid of $(\Delp,L\otimes T)$ if and only if $L$ has no zero divisor; (3) let $\CDp_c$ be the set of all continuous distance distribution functions, then for each continuous t-norm $T$, $(\CDp_c, L\otimes T)$ is a subsemigroup of $(\Delp,L\otimes T)$ if and only if $L$ satisfies the condition $(LS)$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, $(\CDp_c,L\otimes T)$ is an ideal of $(\CDp,L\otimes T)$ if and only if $L$ satisfies the cancellation law.
elements.sent-2411-005: In 1942, Menger introduced the probabilistic metric, in order to describe the distance with some uncertainties between two elements in the space.
elements.sent-2411-006: He replaced the real number $d(p,q)$ by a distance distribution function $F_{pq}$, that is, the distance between the points $p$ and $q$ is a random variable.. In 1962, A.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $\Delp$ be the set of all distance distribution functions.
elements.sent-2411-008: Recall from \cite{Schweizer1983 that a triangle function is a commutative and associative binary operation $\tau$ on $\Delp$ that is increasing in each place and has the top element as identity.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Also in \cite{Schweizer1983 , there are kinds of methods to construct triangle functions.}
elements.sent-2411-010: An important one is generating a triangle function $\tau_{T,L}$ by a triangular norm $T$ on $[0,1]$ and a binary operation $L$ on $[0,\infty]$.
elements.sent-2411-011: The tensor products of quantales afford another method to construct triangle functions \cite{Eklund2018,Gutierrez2017,Gutierrez2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It is observed that the complete lattice $\Delp$ can be treated as the tensor product of $[0,\infty]^{\rm op}$ and $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Then, in particularly, given a left continuous triangular norm $T$ on $[0,1]$ and a right continuous binary operation $L$ on $[0,\infty]$ which is commutative, associative and has $0$ as the identity, we obtain quantales $([0,1],T)$, $([0,\infty]^{\rm op ,L)$ and their tensor product $(\Delp,L\otimes T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Thus, the binary operation $L\otimes T$ becomes a triangle function.
elements.sent-2411-015: It is shown that the binary operation $L\otimes T$ coincides exactly with $\tau_{T,L}$ if $L=+$, see \cite{Eklund2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, in this paper, we show that $L\otimes T$ coincides with $\tau_{T,L}$ if and only if the operation $L$ on $[0,\infty]$ satisfies the condition $(LCS)$.
elements.sent-2411-017: In the literature, see e.g. \cite{Saminger2008,Saminger2010,Schweizer1983 , distance distribution functions being continuous at some points in $[0,\infty]$ are of particular interests.}

elements.sent-2411-019: Usually, the sets $\CDp$, $\CDp_0$ and $\CDp_c$ are required to be closed with respect to the triangle function $L\otimes T$, but it does not always hold.. We show that,.
elements.sent-2411-020: We recall the basics of distance distribution functions in Section 2 and investigate the relationship between the triangle function $\tau_{T,L}$ and $L\otimes T$ in Section 3.
elements.sent-2411-021: Section 4 is devoted to the closedness of the subsets $\CDp$, $\CDp_0$ and $\CDp_c$ with respect to the operation $L\otimes T$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $[0,\infty]$ be the extended non-negative reals, i.e., the set of non-negative real numbers with the symbol $\infty$ added, and ordered by $0\leq x <\infty$ for all $x\in[0,\infty[$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Let $[a,b]$ be a closed interval contained in $[0,\infty]$.
elements.sent-2411-024: The least upper bound of a set $\{x_i:i\in I\}\subseteq[a,b]$ is denoted by $\sup\limits_{i\in I x_i$ and the greatest lower bound is denoted by $\inf\limits_{i\in I x_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Particularly, if the index set $I$ is empty, $\sup_{i\in I x_i=a$ and $\inf\limits_{i\in I x_i=b$.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Let $[a,b]$ and $[c,d]$ be closed intervals contained in $[0,\infty]$.
elements.sent-2411-027: An increasing function $f:[a,b]\lra [c,d]$ is a sup-map if it preserves all suprema, that is, \begin{equation}f(\sup_{i\in I} x_i)=\sup_{i\in I}f(x_i)\end{equation} for all family $\{x_i: i\in I\}$ in $[a,b]$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Particularly, if the index set $I$ is the empty set, one has that $f(a)=c$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Dually, $f$ is an inf-map if it preserves all infima, that is, \begin{equation}f(\inf_{i\in I} x_i)=\inf_{i\in I}f(x_i)\end{equation} for all family $\{x_i: i\in I\}$ in $[a,b]$.
elements.sent-2411-030: If the index set $I$ is the empty set, one has that $f(b)=d$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Given an increasing function $f:[a,b]\lra[c,d]$, define \begin{equation}f^{-}(x)=f(x^{-})=\sup_{y<x} f(y), \quad f^{+}(x)=f(x^{+})=\inf_{y>x}f(y),\end{equation} then $f^-$ is a sup-map and $f^+$ is an inf-map.
elements.sent-2411-032: In fact, $f^-$ is the largest sup-map below $f$ and $f^+$ is the smallest inf-map above $f$.
elements.sent-2411-033: Clearly, $f=f^-$ if and only if $f$ is a sup-map, and $f=f^+$ if and only if $f$ is an inf-map.
elements.sent-2411-034: A distance distribution function (briefly, a d.d.f.) is an increasing function $f:[0,\infty]\lra [0,1]$ satisfies $f(0)=0$ and $f(\infty)=1$, and is left continuous on the open interval $]0,\infty[$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The set of all d.d.f's is denoted by $\Delp$.
elements.sent-2411-037: The set of all non-defective d.d.f's is denoted by $\mathcal{D ^+$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A d.d.f is strict if it is continuous and strictly increasing on $[0,\infty]$.
elements.sent-2411-039: The set of all strict d.d.f's is denoted by $\CDp_{sc}$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Conversely, given a sup-map $g:[0,\infty]\lra[0,1]$, define a function \begin{equation}\widetilde{g}(x)=g(x) x<\infty, 1 x=\infty. \end{equation} Then the function $\widetilde{g}$ is a d.d.f.
elements.sent-2411-041: Clearly, $\widetilde{f^- =f$ for all $f\in\Delp$ and $\widetilde{g ^-={g}$ for all sup-map $g:[0,\infty]\lra[0,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore, $\Delp$ is isomorphic to the set of all sup-maps from $[0,\infty]$ to $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Given a d.d.f $f:[0,\infty]\lra[0,1]$, recall from \cite{Schweizer1983 that the largest quasi-inverse of $f$ is a function \begin{equation}f^\vee:[0,1]\lra[0,\infty],\quad f^\vee(y)=\inf\{x\in[0,\infty]\mid f(x)> y\},\end{equation} the smallest quasi-inverse of $f$ is a function \begin{equation}f^\wedge:[0,1]\lra[0,\infty], \quad f^\wedge(y)=\sup\{x\in[0,\infty]\mid f(x)<y\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Consequently, $f$ is continuous on an open interval $]a,b[$ if and only if it is increasing strictly on the interval $]f(a^+),f(b^-)[$.

elements.sent-2411-052: A map $\vphi:M_1\times M_2\lra M_3$ is a bi-sup-map if it preserves suprema in each place, that is, \begin{equation}\vphi(\sup_{i\in I}x_i,y)=\sup_{i\in I}\vphi(x_i,y),\quad \vphi(x,\sup_{i\in I}y_i)=\sup_{i\in I}\vphi(x,y_i).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-055: The interval $[0,\infty]$ equipped with the opposite of the usual order is also a complete lattice, denoted by $\Rop$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Notice that the complete lattice $\Rop$ has the top element $0$ and the bottom element $\infty$.

elements.section-2411-003: Triangle functions generated by t-norms and $\CL$-operations
elements.sent-2411-060: A t-norm $T$ is called to be left continuous (or continuous) if $T: [0,1]^2\lra [0,1]$ is a left continuous (or continuous) function with respect to the usual topology on $[0,1]$.
elements.sent-2411-061: An $\CL$-operation is an associative binary operation $L$ on $[0,\infty]$ that is commutative, increasing in each place, and has $0$ as identity.
elements.sent-2411-062: An $\CL$-operation $L$ is right continuous (or continuous) if the function $L:[0,\infty]^2\lra[0,\infty]$ is right continuous (or continuous) with respect to the usual topology on $[0,\infty]$.
